summary: Crystal‑clear Charlie Miller soundboard capture of a June ’76 show that erupts after a tentative first set, delivering a legendary St Stephen→Eyes→Let It Grow sequence and a soaring Crazy Fingers.
review: The recording is an excellent soundboard mix with just a short audience patch at the start of St. Stephen that adds historic ambience while keeping every instrument pristine. Musically the band warms up around Scarlet Begonias and then unleashes a second set that many fans rank among the year’s best, highlighted by Crazy Fingers, the expansive St. Stephen‑Eyes‑Let It Grow suite, and an emotional Franklin’s Tower encore.

ai_rating.rationale: Overwhelmingly positive comments on both performance (many 5‑star praises of key songs) and recording quality (multiple mentions of crisp, clean mix). The few lower ratings are outliers.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Crystal‑clear mix with balanced levels; a brief audience patch at the very start of St. Stephen adds atmosphere but does not compromise clarity. No noticeable dropouts or distortion.

standout_songs: St. Stephen > Eyes > Let It Grow
show_quality.setlist_flow: First set starts a bit slow, finding its groove around Scarlet Begonias; the second set bursts into high‑energy improvisation and never lets up, delivering one of the most memorable mid‑70s runs.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Guitar tone praised as perfect and relaxed; vocals noted a bit low but overall his lead work is conversational and inspiring.
band_member_comments.Phil: Bass highlighted repeatedly for funky, driving lines (especially on Eyes) and for being prominently mixed and “inspired” throughout.
band_member_comments.Bob: Rhythm guitar and vocal contributions described as solid; the "fly‑away" section of Wharf Rat called "exactly perfect".
band_member_comments.Keys: Keyboard work praised for tasteful solos and smooth integration with the band; trade‑offs with Jerry and Bobby noted as a highlight.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill’s drumming lauded, especially his aggressive intro on Let It Grow; overall drum performance described as “ruling” the jams.
